nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef flats, tide pools, surge zones, and fore-reef rock faces — typically found wedged into self-excavated burrows on basaltic and calcareous rock substrates. Emerges at night to graze algae from surrounding surfaces.

decorPreferences: abundant mature live rock for grazing and shelter
stable rockwork securely braced against burrowing/wedging movement
caves and crevices for daytime hiding
carbonate (limestone) live rock that the urchin can bore into

diet.notes: Algae grazer — emerges at night to feed on algal films, turf algae, and macroalgae across live rock and glass. Generally accepts dried seaweed (nori) clipped to the rockwork and sinking algae wafers when natural algae growth is insufficient. Has been documented eating coralline algae as well, so it may thin coralline in tanks with limited grazing surface.

goodWith: stony and soft corals (does not consume coral tissue)
other peaceful invertebrates with separate territory
avoidWith: urchin predators (large triggerfish, pufferfish, large wrasses, harlequin tuskfish)
tanks built on unstable or lightweight rockwork that can be undermined
show tanks where coralline algae must be preserved on visible surfaces

summary: The Rock-Boring Urchin (Echinometra mathaei) is an Indo-Pacific sea urchin that grinds cavities into live rock using its spines and jaws. Widely considered a peaceful, hardy nocturnal algae grazer, though its burrowing habit can erode aquascape rock and destabilize structures over time.
careNotes: Important warning: this species ACTIVELY EXCAVATES BURROWS in live rock. The Rock-Boring Urchin uses its spines and Aristotle's lantern (the multi-part jaw apparatus common to sea urchins) to grind out cavities in calcareous or basaltic rock, where it wedges itself during the day and continues enlarging these burrows as it grows. In a reef aquarium this can be destructive: the urchin will reshape live rock, dislodge coral frags glued nearby, and over time can undermine rock pillars to the point of collapse. Mount frags well away from any urchin retreats, and confirm rockwork is bottom-supported (or epoxied/pinned) so that bore-out cavities at the base cannot cause a column to topple. The Caribbean species Echinometra lucunter shares essentially the same trade common name, burrowing habit, and care requirements; if a specimen is sourced from the Western Atlantic it is almost certainly E. lucunter rather than E. mathaei. Generally considered reef-safe in the strict sense (does not consume coral tissue) and widely valued as a nocturnal algae grazer that mows down turf and film algae on rockwork and glass. Has been reported to graze coralline algae as well, which can thin visible coralline coverage over time. Active mainly at night; expect to see it tucked into a rock crevice during daylight. The Rock-Boring Urchin is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to elevated nitrate — poor water quality has been reported to cause spine loss and lethargy. Like other echinoderms, it is intolerant of rapid salinity changes; any top-off or water-change procedures should drip-acclimate the urchin slowly. Handle with care — the spines are sharp and have been reported to break off under the skin when grasped; use gloves or a soft net. Best added to mature, biologically established tanks with stable, well-cycled rockwork and a steady algae supply; in newly set-up or sterile tanks, supplement the diet with dried seaweed.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source — Shortspine Urchin (Echinometra sp.) product page. Confirmed family Echinometridae, peaceful temperament, reef compatible, herbivore diet, care level easy, 20 gal minimum, max 3" size, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.023-1.025, nocturnal algae grazer that burrows niches in carbonate rock, copper-sensitive, handle-with-care spines.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Designated secondary source — Bulk Reef Supply does not carry a product page for Echinometra mathaei / Shortspine Urchin (search returned no urchin products). No cross-confirmation possible from this source; swapped to Wikipedia and Waikiki Aquarium for cross-source verification.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Echinometra mathaei)
additional.notes: Taxonomic and ecological cross-source — confirmed family Echinometridae, order Camarodonta, distribution from Red Sea/Madagascar to Hawaii, depth range to 139 m, test diameter ~5 cm, burrowing behavior using spines and teeth to excavate rock cavities, nocturnal algae diet, bioerosion impact on reefs.

additional.name: Waikīkī Aquarium — Rock-boring Urchin
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed adult size to 6 cm test diameter, Indo-Pacific distribution including Hawaii, tide-pool and reef-flat habitat in wave-swept surge zones, burrows enlarged by spines and scraping jaws as the animal grows, herbivorous diet of algal film, tube-feet food-transport mechanism.
